options.text: London is more bigger than Sfax.
Anis is taller than his cousin.
The tea was gooder than the coffee.
Bizerte is more small than London.

questions.explanation: "Taller than" correctly forms the comparative of a short adjective ✓. "More bigger" doubles the comparison, "gooder" ignores the irregular "better", and "more small" should be "smaller" (short adjective → -er).

questions.prompt: Which sentence about the picnic is correct?

options.text: While they were eating, a dog stole the sandwich.
While they ate, a dog was stealing the sandwich.
While they were eating, a dog was stole the sandwich.
While they eating, a dog stole the sandwich.

questions.explanation: The background action after "while" is past continuous ("were eating"), and the short event that cuts in is past simple ("stole"): sentence a ✓. Sentence b swaps the tenses, c writes "was stole" (never be + past simple), and d forgets "were" before "eating".

questions.prompt: Anis wrote: "Bizerte is smaller than London." This means…

options.text: Bizerte is bigger than London.
London is bigger than Bizerte.
London is smaller than Bizerte.
Bizerte and London are the same size.

questions.explanation: "Bizerte is smaller than London" means London is the bigger of the two, so "London is bigger than Bizerte" ✓. Options a and c reverse the relationship, and d denies the difference the sentence states.

questions.prompt: One sentence has a wrong verb form. Which one?

options.text: The Bakers were watching TV at 9 p.m.
Anis was writing a postcard to his mother.
The birds was singing in the garden.
We were waiting for the bus.

questions.explanation: "The birds" is plural, so it needs "were", not "was": it should be "The birds were singing" ✓. The other three correctly match was (singular) or were (plural) with the -ing form.

questions.prompt: Anis wrote: "The old part of London has narrow streets and ancient houses." Which word means "very, very old"?

options.text: narrow
modern
ancient
crowded

questions.explanation: "Ancient" means very, very old, from long ago ✓. "Narrow" means not wide, "modern" means new (the opposite), and "crowded" means full of people.

questions.prompt: Complete: "Anis ___ tired at the end of the day, but he thought London was far ___ than he expected."

options.text: wasn't … more beautiful
didn't … more beautiful
wasn't … beautifuller
weren't … more beautiful

questions.explanation: The past negative of "be" with a single subject is "wasn't" ("Anis wasn't tired"), and "beautiful" is a long adjective → "more beautiful than": option a ✓. "Didn't" would need a main verb, "weren't" is plural, and "beautifuller" breaks the long-adjective rule.
